summary: Two of three claims hold. The middle claim inverts the role of Reader and Resolver, which is the most common failure mode for §4.2.
original_answer: The Reader must not have MCP access because it writes the final output, so it could leak data.

claims.text: The Reader must not have MCP access.
claims.label: Policy boundary
claims.rationale: Matches §4.2: the Reader tier is explicitly denied tool access at the policy boundary because its input is fully untrusted.

source_span.quote: The Reader is intentionally denied MCP tool access. This is a hard policy, not a default.

claims.text: The Reader writes the final output.
claims.label: Role inversion
claims.rationale: The Resolver writes final outputs. The Reader only ingests and structures untrusted documents — it has no write surface to the user.
claims.improvement: State that the Resolver writes user-visible output, while the Reader is read-only on untrusted input.

source_span.quote: the user-visible answer is always written by the Resolver.

claims.label: Vague mechanism
claims.rationale: True in spirit, vague in mechanism. The chapter is specific: prompt injection inside an untrusted document could turn into an external tool call if the Reader had MCP access.
claims.improvement: Name the mechanism: prompt injection → tool call → exfiltration.

source_span.quote: an injected instruction could become an external action — a request to a database, a webhook, an outbound email.

missing_ideas: Splitting capabilities beats hardening a single model.
The trust boundary lives between Reader and Planner, not inside any one tier.
suggested_revision: The Reader is denied MCP access because it reads untrusted documents. If it had MCP access, prompt injection could make it call external systems or exfiltrate data. The Resolver, not the Reader, writes final outputs.
next_retrieval_prompt: In two days: Without naming the tiers, describe the architectural property that prevents prompt-injection exfiltration in a 3-tier agent.
